coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical biosensor for 1,4-dihydroxybenzene based on copper sulfide and horseradish peroxidase enzyme: Application in skin cream samples
coredata.dc:creator: da Silva Freires, André
Botelho, Chirlene Nascimento
Silva, Saimon Moraes
Goulart, Marília Oliveira Fonseca
Damos, Flavio Santos
Luz, Rita de Cássia Silva
coredata.dc:description: This work presents the development and application of an ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical biosensor based on copper (I) sulfide (Cu2S) and horseradish peroxidase enzyme (HRP) immobilized on a fluorine-doped tin oxide modified glass slide (FTO) for the determination of 1,4-dihydroxybenzene (DHB). The Cu2S/FTO photoelectrochemical platform was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The electrochemical properties of the proposed photoelectrochemical biosensor (HRP-Cu2S/FTO) were evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and amperometry. The photocurrent of the photoelectrochemical platform was amperometrically monitored, using a commercial 20 W white LED light bulb as the source of light. After optimization of experimental parameters, the biosensor had shown good responses for detection of DHB, in the presence of 50 µmol L−1 of hydrogen peroxide, exhibiting a wide linear range for DHB concentrations between 10 nmol L−1 up to 1 mmol L−1 (r 2 = 0.998), with a detection limit of 4.0 nmol L−1. The selectivity of the photoelectrochemical biosensor was also evaluated and the results demonstrated that the proposed biosensor presents a good selectivity for DHB detection. The HRP-Cu2S/FTO photoelectrochemical biosensor was applied in skin cream samples presenting recovery percentages for the analyte in the samples between 99.89 and 100.70%, suggesting a good accuracy for the proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper (I) sulfide
Horseradish peroxidase
Photoelectrochemical biosensor
1,4-dihydroxybenzene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor
Application of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor in skin cream samples and determination of DHB in the samples by a comparative method
Characterization of the PEC platforms by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
Photoelectrochemical behavior of the sensor and optimization of experimental and operational parameters of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO biosensor response toward DHB
Analytical performance of the PEC biosensor for the determination of DHB
Application of the PEC biosensor for the DHB determination in skin cream samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Cient?fico e Tecnol?gico ? CNPq (Universal, Processes: 421139/2016 and 426337/2016-6; Produtividade, Processes: 303525/2016 and 308204/2018-2), Funda??o de Amparo ? Pesquisa e ao Desenvolvimento Cient?fico e Tecnol?gico do Maranh?o ? FAPEMA (Processes INFRA-03186/18, Universal-01194/17) and INCTBio, Instituto Nacional de Ci?ncia e Tecnologia em Bioanal?tica ? Brasil, for financial support. The authors are grateful to the Laborat?rio de Caracteriza??o e Microscopia de Materiais - Grupo de ?ptica e Nanoscopia (Universidade Federal de Alagoas, Macei?, Al, Brazil) for the SEM images and EDS measurements. We thank to Ana Rubia Ribeiro for carrying out the SEM measurements.

The authors are grateful to Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico – CNPq (Universal, Processes: 421139/2016 and 426337/2016-6; Produtividade, Processes: 303525/2016 and 308204/2018-2), Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa e ao Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico do Maranhão – FAPEMA (Processes INFRA-03186/18, Universal-01194/17) and INCTBio, Instituto Nacional de Ciência e Tecnologia em Bioanalítica – Brasil, for financial support. The authors are grateful to the Laboratório de Caracterização e Microscopia de Materiais - Grupo de Óptica e Nanoscopia (Universidade Federal de Alagoas, Maceió, Al, Brazil) for the SEM images and EDS measurements. We thank to Ana Rubia Ribeiro for carrying out the SEM measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scanning electron microscopy image (SEM) and (b) energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) obtained for Cu2S/FTO. (c) Nyquist plots performed in solution 0.1 mol L−1 of KCl containing 5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− for the FTO modified with Cu2S (Cu2S/FTO) (blue circles) and the FTO modified with HRP-Cu2S (HRP-Cu2S/FTO) (black circles). (d) Nyquist plot for the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor, recorded in 0.1 mol L−1 of KCl containing 5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in the absence of LED light illumination (black circles) and under visible LED light illumination (blue circles). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) PEC response of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO electrode in: PBS (black line), PBS containing H2O2 (red line) and PBS containing H2O2 and DHB (blue line). The pH value of the PBS buffer was 7.0. (b) Schematic representation proposed for PEC detection of DHB with the HRP-Cu2S/FTO biosensor in presence of H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PEC response of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO biosensor for: (a) different pH values of PBS containing 100 μmol L−1 of H2O2 and 200 μmol L-1 of DHB; (b) different buffer solutions (0.1 mol L−1), containing 100 μmol L −1 of H2O2 and 200 μmol L−1 DHB; (c) different concentrations of H2O2 containing 200 μmol L−1 of DHB in 0.1 mol L−1 HBS, pH 6.5; (d) applied potential, containing 50 μmol L−1 of H2O2 and 200 μmol L−1 of DHB in 0.1 mol L−1 HBS, pH 6.5.

(a) Amperograms obtained using different concentrations (0 µmol L−1; 0.01 µmol L−1; 0.1 µmol L−1; 1.0 µmol L−1; 10.0 µmol L−1; 100.0 µmol L−1; 1000 µmol L−1) of DHB in 0.1 mol L−1 of PBS, pH 6.5, [H2O2] = 50 μmol L−1; (b) Analytical curve for the data obtained in Fig. 4a. (c) Influence of possible interferences on the photocurrent intensity of DHB: (1) DHB + catechol; (2) DHB + cysteamine; (3) DHB + ascorbic acid; (4) DHB + 4-hydroxybenzoic acid propyl ester, (5) DHB + sodium disulfite, (6) DHB + dodecyl sodium sulfate and (7) DHB + citric acid. Experiments were carried out in 0.1 mol L−1 HBS, pH 6.5.
Comparison of analytical parameters of this present method with different reports from the literature.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviation list: DPV, differential pulse voltammetry; PEC, photoelectrochemistry; ECL, electrochemiluminescence; QD, quantum dots; FI-AMP, flow injection amperometry; SPCE-CNT, screen printed modified with carbon nanotubes; CuO-His-GQD, copper oxide-histidine functionalized graphene quantum dots hybrid; GNPs/ITO, gold nanoparticle-modified indium tin oxide; MoS2/RGO, MoS2/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites; N/S/P co-doped CDs, N/S/P-co-doped carbon dots; Co3O4-His-GQD, Co3O4 and histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots; Fe2O3/CNTs/FTO; SWCNTs, Single walled carbon nanotubes. CuCNFs-Lac, copper, carbon nanofibers and laccase; GCE, glassy carbon electrode; SiNWs, silicon nanowires; AuNPs, gold nanoparticles; GQDs, graphene quantum dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of the determination of DHB in skin cream samples (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Values obtained after considering the dilution factor

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical biosensor for 1,4-dihydroxybenzene based on copper sulfide and horseradish peroxidase enzyme: Application in skin cream samples

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Federal University of Maranhão, São Luís, Brazil

Faculty of Science, Engineering and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents the development and application of an ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical biosensor based on copper (I) sulfide (Cu2S) and horseradish peroxidase enzyme (HRP) immobilized on a fluorine-doped tin oxide modified glass slide (FTO) for the determination of 1,4-dihydroxybenzene (DHB). The Cu2S/FTO photoelectrochemical platform was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The electrochemical properties of the proposed photoelectrochemical biosensor (HRP-Cu2S/FTO) were evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and amperometry. The photocurrent of the photoelectrochemical platform was amperometrically monitored, using a commercial 20 W white LED light bulb as the source of light. After optimization of experimental parameters, the biosensor had shown good responses for detection of DHB, in the presence of 50 µmol L−1 of hydrogen peroxide, exhibiting a wide linear range for DHB concentrations between 10 nmol L−1 up to 1 mmol L−1 (r 2 = 0.998), with a detection limit of 4.0 nmol L−1. The selectivity of the photoelectrochemical biosensor was also evaluated and the results demonstrated that the proposed biosensor presents a good selectivity for DHB detection. The HRP-Cu2S/FTO photoelectrochemical biosensor was applied in skin cream samples presenting recovery percentages for the analyte in the samples between 99.89 and 100.70%, suggesting a good accuracy for the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:para: 1,4-Dihydroxybenzene (DHB), also known as hydroquinone, is a phenolic compound commonly used in paper manufacturing, photographic developing, and cosmetic products as skin lotions, gel and creams [1–4]. This compound has been widely applied in the field of cosmetics as a strong depigmentation agent, carrying the main function of inhibiting melanin production [5]. Its use as a skin whitening agent, effectively occurs, when in the range from 2 to 5% w/w [6,7], in the pharmaceutical formulations. DHB above 5% could cause local irritation [8] and even persistent hypopigmentation named leukoderma [9]. Other reported dermatological complications of DHB include cataracts, scleral disorders, nail pigmentation and patchy depigmentation [10]. Therefore, over the past years DHB has become a controversial skin-care agent for topical use. It should be mentioned that the whitening products containing DHB have been banned in many countries, due to cancer risk related concerns [10]. Therefore, the presence of DHB in certain concentrations in cosmetic products may cause serious damages to health. Thus, the US Food and Drug Administration (US FDA) allows a maximum of 2 to 5% DHB in cosmetic products [11].

In this context, several efforts have been done towards developing methodologies for the detection of DHB [12–21]. Electrochemical methods stand out among the most prominent methods for DHB determination due to its low cost, high sensitivity and portability. The DHB determination by electrochemical approaches involves the use of modified electrodes, using carbon nanofibers [15], carbon nanotubes [21], nanoparticles [18], mesoporous carbon [20], graphene [14,19], and metallic complexes [12]. Electrochemical approaches show high versatility, since it can be performed by monitoring the potential or the electrical current by many different techniques including cyclic voltammetry, amperometry [22], differential pulse voltammetry [23,24], AC voltammetry and square wave voltammetry.

The photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor is an emerging electrochemical-based approach for general quantification of molecules [25–28]. PEC sensors combine the electrochemical detection to light photo-excitation of carriers in semiconductors [26,29]. Thus, PEC methods retain the versatility of electrochemical methods with advantage of a high signal-to-noise ratio, once the excitation source does not promote electrical noise on the detector. Thus, PEC sensors typically present low noise signal and consequently high sensitivity, owing to the efficient separation of the excitation light source from the detected signal [26,30].

Herein, we propose an enzymatic PEC biosensor for DHB quantification based on copper (I) sulfide (Cu2S) and horseradish peroxidase enzyme (HRP), immobilized on fluorine-doped tin oxide modified glass slides (FTO). The motivation of using the semiconductor material, Cu2S, as a platform to immobilize HRP onto the electrode surface was due to its low energy band gap, good biocompatibility, high electron transfer rate and good electrical conductivity (1 mS cm−1) [31,32]. Additionally, Cu2S has demonstrated to display metal-like electrical conductivity, a desirable characteristic in PEC sensors [31]. On the other hand, HRP enzyme is the most commonly used enzyme for practical analytical applications, such as construction of biosensors for the detection of molecules, due to its high stability [33].

Thus, the use of copper (I) sulfide (Cu2S) and horseradish peroxidase enzyme (HRP) combined to PEC transduction introduces advantages such as simple instrumentation, low cost, portability, selectivity, stability, fast response, being more advantageous than many other analytical methodologies for the determination of DHB [27]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first enzymatic PEC sensor for detection of DHB in cosmetic samples, exploiting HRP-Cu2S and a low cost visible LED light as an excitation source.

All reagents used in this work were of analytical grade. Copper sulfate, DHB, catechol, cysteamine, ascorbic acid, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid propyl ester, sodium disulfite, and dodecyl sodium sulfate, chitosan, glutaraldehyde, 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinoethanesulfonic (HBS or HEPES), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (São Paulo, Brazil). Sodium thiosulfate, monobasic sodium phosphate, citric acid, and potassium chloride, were purchased from ISOFAR (Rio de Janeiro, Brazil). Macllvaine buffer solution (MCV), also known as citrate–phosphate buffer, was prepared from citric acid and sodium phosphate. All solutions were prepared with purified water in an OS100LXE system, obtained from the GEHAKA Company (Gehaka Ltd., São Paulo, SP, Brazil).

PEC measurements were performed using a PGSTAT204 potentiostat/galvanostat from Metrohm controlled by Nova 2.0 Software connected to a cell containing three electrodes: a glass plate covered with fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) used as a working electrode; a gold wire, used as a counter electrode and the Ag/AgCl (saturated) electrode as a reference electrode. Photoamperograms were obtained turning the light on every ten seconds. The photocurrent values were sampled five seconds after the light is turned on, in order to construct the analytical curve.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed with a PGSTAT 128 N potentiostat/galvanostat equipped with a FRA 2 module from Metrohm controlled by the FRA software. All electrochemical measurements were performed in degassed solutions (solutions were purged with nitrogen gas). Impedance spectra were obtained at 0.1 mol L−1 KCl solution containing 5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- in a frequency range of 10 MHz − 0.1 MHz, under an AC amplitude of 10 mV. The effect of light on the values of resistance to the charge transfer was evaluated in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl solution containing 5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. A commercial visible 20 W white LED light bulb was used as the source of light in the photoelectrochemical studies. Morphological characteristics of Cu2S/FTO platform were investigated using Superscan SSX-550 model (Shimadzu) scanning electron microscope (SEM). Furthermore, the elemental composition of Cu2S film was investigated by means of energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), by using an attachment of SEM instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the FTO electrode was immersed in ethanol, sonicated for 15 min to remove any adsorbed species and then washed with distilled water. Electrodeposition of Cu2S was performed by adapting previously reported methods for copper (I) sulfide electrodeposition [34,35]. Briefly, 10 mL of an aqueous solution containing 400 mmol L−1 Na2S2O3 and 10 mmol L−1 CuSO4 was prepared and the pH was adjusted to 2.5. The electrodeposition of Cu2S was performed by using chronoamperometry, with an applied potential of −0.8 V, during 360 s. Subsequently, a 10 μL aliquot of the aqueous solution, containing 4 mg mL−1 of HRP, 1% glutaraldehyde and 0.15% chitosan was added to the surface of the Cu2S/FTO platform and left to dry for 12 h at 4 °C. BSA (1% w/v) was added to the modified electrode surface to block the possible remaining active sites on the platform surface, avoiding any nonspecific adsorption. Finally, the HRP modified Cu2S/FTO platform (HRP-Cu2S/FTO) was washed with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor in skin cream samples and determination of DHB in the samples by a comparative method
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, two samples of skin creams (Samples A and B) were obtained from a local drugstore and prepared by adapting a previously reported method for DHB determination [36]. One gram of each sample was dissolved in 20 mL of 0.05 mol L−1 sulfuric acid and the solution was shaken with the aid of an ultrasonic bath. Each solution was then transferred into 25 mL standard volumetric flask and the volume was adjusted with 0.05 mol L−1 sulfuric acid. The solutions (A and B) were then filtered. An aliquot of 100 µL was removed from the solution containing sample A and it was added to the PEC cell containing 5 mL of the electrolyte for the determination of DHB. For the sample B, 50 µL were removed from the solution containing sample B and added to the PEC cell. To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method for the determination of DHB, the results obtained by the PEC method were compared to a spectrophotometric method [36]. Thus, the concentration of DHB in samples (A and B) was also determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Model 2048, from Avantes) at a wavelength of 290 nm using a quartz cuvette of 10 mm optical path length.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the PEC platforms by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the morphology observed with (a) scanning electron microscopy SEM and (b) energy Dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) for a Cu2S film electrodeposited on FTO by potentiostatic conditions. As can be seen, the Cu2S nanostructures were homogeneously distributed on the surface of the FTO electrodes, resulting in a uniform coverage of the surface. The EDS for elemental peaks of Cu2S electrodeposited on FTO electrode shows the presence of Cu and S. The atomic percentage of Cu element was higher than that of S (Fig. 1b), indicating the presence of Cu2S successfully synthesized by the electrodeposition method [37,38]. The EDS also presented the signals of Sn and O from SnO2 in FTO substrate.

EIS was used to evaluate the resistance to charge transfer of HRP-Cu2S/FTO and Cu2S/FTO PEC platforms. These platforms were prepared according to the experimental procedures described in section 2.3. As can be seen, Fig. 1c shows that the Cu2S/FTO PEC sensor presents a semicircle significantly smaller than the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor, suggesting that immobilization of the heme-containing glycoprotein increases resistance to charge transfer between the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe and PEC platform.

In order to evaluate the effect of the light on the response of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor, Nyquist plots in presence (blue spectrum) and in absence of light (black spectrum) were obtained in a 0.1 mol L−1 of KCl solution, containing 5 mmol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4, as shown in Fig. 1d. As can be seen, the impedance spectrum presents a larger semicircle, in the absence of light, when compared to the plot obtained in its presence. The incidence of light on the HRP-Cu2S/FTO platform results in photogenerated carriers, which can be driven by the potential drop at the space charge layer, contributing to the decrease of charge transfer resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical behavior of the sensor and optimization of experimental and operational parameters of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO biosensor response toward DHB
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the effect of the hydrogen peroxide on the response of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO biosensor, measurements of the photocurrent in 0.1 mol L−1 phosphate buffer solution (PBS), pH 7.0, with an applied potential of −0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl, were performed. Fig. 2 a shows the amperometric responses for the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor recorded in PBS (black line), in PBS containing 100 μmol L−1 of H2O2 (red line), in PBS containing 100 μmol L−1 of H2O2 and 100 μmol L−1 of DHB (blue line).

As can be observed in Fig. 2a (black line), the HRP-Cu2S/FTO presented an average photocurrent value of −10.41 (±0.42) µA, due to the reduction of water [39]. In order to evaluate the effects of dissolved oxygen, the photocurrent of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO was evaluated in absence and in presence of oxygen. The photocurrent of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC platform was not affected by the presence of oxygen (data not shown). On the other hand, addition of H2O2 to the buffer solution resulted in a photocurrent increase, to −26.51 (±0.98) µA (red line), when compared to the HRP-Cu2S/FTO electrode, in buffer solution. Thus, the photocurrent of the platform is sensitive to the presence of H2O2 due to its interaction with the HRP enzyme, according to the following reaction:
(1a) HRP(FeIII, P) + H2O2 → Compound I [HRP(FeIV = O, P+•)] + H2O
(1b)
Compound
I
H R P
F
e
IV
= O ,
P
+ ·
+ 2
e
C
u 2
S
-
+ 2
H +
→
electrode
H R P
F
e
III
, P
+
H 2
O

where HRP (FeIII, P) represents the native ferric enzyme HRP and HRP (FeIV = O, P+•) is the oxidized intermediate, designed as Compound I. The equation (Eq. (1a)) represents the two-electron oxidation process of the active site of the enzyme by H2O2 and consequent formation of Compound I [40,41]. In the next step (Eq. (1b)), Compound I is reduced via two electrons by the electrons from the conduction band of Cu2S in HRP-Cu2S/FTO electrode, regenerating HRP (FeIII, P). On the other hand, the addition of DHB to the PEC cell (blue line) gives rise to an electron mediated transfer, in which the phenolic compound competes with the electrode in reducing Compound I, to produce the native form of the enzyme (HRP(FeIII, P)) according to the following reactions [42,43].
(1c) Compound I [HRP (FeIV = O, P+•)] + DHB → HRP (FeIII, P) + BQ (benzoquinone)

In equation (1c), Compound I reacts with DHB, regenerating the enzyme in its native form [HRP (FeIII, P)] plus benzoquinone (BQ) [44]. Thus, the addition of DHB in the PEC cell resulted in a decrease of the amperometric response of the biosensor, due to the decrease of the surface concentration of Compound I (blue line), indicating that the concentration of DHB, in the medium, can be monitored/determined by the decrease of the biosensor photocurrent. Therefore, DHB introduces a competitive mechanism via mediated electron transfer, in which, possibly the Compound I, chemically generated by H2O2, is consumed by the added DHB. Thus, the photocurrent resulting from the direct reduction of Compound I to the native form of HRP (Eq. (1b)), on the electrode is decreased. Despite the mediated electron transfer, which results in the production of ortho-benzoquinone, its electroreduction back to the original compound and further electrooxidation on the oxide tin platforms present a slow kinetics [45], which can contribute to a more difficult direct reduction of benzoquinone on the surface of the PEC platform. Based on this fact, Fig. 2b displays a proposed scheme for the PEC detection of the DHB on the HRP-Cu2S/FTO film.

In order to establish the best experimental conditions for the PEC determination of DHB, the effects of solution pH, nature of the buffer solution, applied potential and H2O2 concentration were studied (Fig. 3 ). The pH value is an important parameter to be considered on the photocurrent of the PEC platform, since the biosensor performance relies on the activity of HRP, as well as on the pH, which can influence some properties of PEC platforms. Fig. 3a shows the effect of pH on the photocurrent of HRP-Cu2S/FTO platform in 0.1 mol L−1 of phosphate buffer, with pH values ranging from 5.0 to 8.0 in the presence of 100 μmol L−1 of H2O2 and 200 μmol L−1 of DHB, under an applied potential of −0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl. According to the Fig. 3a, it is observed that the PEC response of the biosensor increases from pH 5.0 to 6.5, and it shows a slight decrease from pH 6.5 up to pH 8.0. It is known that strongly acid or alkaline environments can cause denaturation of enzymes. Thus, considering the stability of the enzyme and a more efficient reduction of the peroxide, a pH value of 6.5 was chosen for further experiments.

In order to evaluate the effect of the nature of the buffer on the PEC performance of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO sensor for H2O2 and DHB molecules, three different electrolytic solutions at the concentration of 0.1 mol L−1 and pH 6.5 were prepared HBS, McIlvaine (MCV) and phosphate (PBS). Among the studied buffers (Fig. 3b), it was observed that the HBS presented the higher photocurrent responses, in comparison to the other buffers. Thus, HBS was chosen as a buffer solution in further experiments. The influence of H2O2 concentration on the response of the PEC biosensor is presented in Fig. 3c. Thus, the effects of the following H2O2 concentrations (25, 50, 100 and 200 μmol L−1) were evaluated for a fixed concentration of 200 μmol L−1 DHB in 0.1 mol L−1 of HBS buffer, pH 6.5. It was observed that the highest photocurrent value is obtained for a H2O2 concentration of 50 μmol L−1. Based on these results, the concentration 50 μmol L−1 of H2O2 was chosen to perform the following experiments.

Finally, the applied potential to the working electrode was also studied, once it can significantly influence the analytical performance of the sensor. In this sense, the photocurrents for potentials ranging from −0.1 up to −0.4 V were evaluated in 0.1 mol L−1 of HBS (pH 6.5) containing 50 μmol L−1 of H2O2 and 200 μmol L−1 of DHB (Fig. 3d). It was observed that the photocurrent increases from −0.1 to −0.2 V, having a significant decrease above this potential up to −0.4 V. Thus, the potential of PEC platform was applied at −0.2 V, in further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the PEC biosensor for the determination of DHB
ce:section.ce:para:
After the optimization of the experimental and operational parameters, the analytical performance of the HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor was evaluated monitoring the shift of the photocurrent (ΔI = I 0 - I n) by successive additions of DHB aliquots in HBS 0.1 mol L−1, pH 6.5, in the presence of 50 μmol L−1 of H2O2. I 0 and I n are the photocurrents before and after the addition of DHB to the supporting electrolyte, in the electrochemical cell. As can be seen from the results presented in Fig. 4 a, there is a decrease of the photocurrent with the increase of the DHB concentration, in the electrochemical cell. The calibration curve (Fig. 4b) shows a linear relationship between the decrease of the photocurrent and the log of the DHB concentration, at a concentration range of 0.01 up to 1.000 µmol L−1 (or 10 nmol L−1 up to 1 mmol L−1). The regression equation obtained (ΔI / μA = -31.67 – 3.83 log [DHB] / mol L−1) presented a linear regression coefficient of 0.998 and the detection limit estimated was equal to 4.0 nmol L−1 obtained from the expression 3σbl, where σbl is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 10). The analytical performance of HRP-Cu2S/FTO PEC biosensor was compared to other works reported in the literature for the determination of DHB (Table 1
[46–57]). The proposed method shows lower detection limit and wider linear response range for the DHB detection.

The effects of some possible interfering molecules commonly found in cream samples that contain DHB were evaluated on the response of the PEC biosensor. In this study (Fig. 4c), the photocurrent obtained for a fixed concentration of DHB (10 µmol L−1) was compared to the photocurrents obtained, when species such as (1) catechol, (2) cysteamine, (3) ascorbic acid, (4) 4-hydroxybenzoic acid propyl ester, (5) sodium disulfite, (6) citric acid, and (7) dodecyl sodium sulfate were present in solution in a concentration 10 times higher (1 mmol L−1) than the DHB concentration. According to Fig. 4c, the percentage of response of the present biosensor for DHB has not presented significant changes, when in the absence and presence of interfering compounds. The percentage values found for the DHB photocurrents in absence and presence of interfering species are very close, with differences between their values lower than 5%. Therefore, these results demonstrate that the determination of DHB with the PEC biosensor is not significantly influenced by the tested compounds, indicating a good selectivity for the determination of this analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the PEC biosensor for the DHB determination in skin cream samples
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of DHB in skin cream samples was performed employing the standard addition method and the results are presented in Table 2 . As can be seen, the quantities of DHB determined in cream samples were of 18.76 (±0.06) mg and 37.52 (±0.10) mg for samples A and B, respectively, after considering the dilution factor for each sample. The amounts recovered in the samples were very close to the concentration value of DHB obtained with spectrophotometric method, suggesting good accuracy for the proposed sensor (Table 2).

The present work shows that the combined use of Cu2S and HRP is a promising strategy for the construction of PEC biosensor for the determination of DHB, exploiting the chemical effects of H2O2 on HRP performance. In the absence of DHB, the system is represented by a direct electron transfer between HRP and H2O2. Upon the addition of DHB, a mediated electron transfer occurs, which competes with the direct electron transfer. Thus, an intermediate Compound I is formed by chemical reaction between HRP and H2O2, which reacts with DHB to regenerate the native form of the enzyme and DHB. Thus, the photoelectrochemical method is able to monitor the concentration of DHB. The proposed sensor presents a wide linear response range for DHB, being an appropriate device for DHB determination, in skin cream samples.

André da Silva Freires: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Chirlene Nascimento Botelho: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Saimon Moraes Silva: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Marília Oliveira Fonseca Goulart: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Flavio Santos Damos: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Rita de Cássia Silva Luz: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico – CNPq (Universal, Processes: 421139/2016 and 426337/2016-6; Produtividade, Processes: 303525/2016 and 308204/2018-2), Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa e ao Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico do Maranhão – FAPEMA (Processes INFRA-03186/18, Universal-01194/17) and INCTBio, Instituto Nacional de Ciência e Tecnologia em Bioanalítica – Brasil, for financial support. The authors are grateful to the Laboratório de Caracterização e Microscopia de Materiais - Grupo de Óptica e Nanoscopia (Universidade Federal de Alagoas, Maceió, Al, Brazil) for the SEM images and EDS measurements. We thank to Ana Rubia Ribeiro for carrying out the SEM measurements.
